flashcards.front: Why is atomicAdd a bottleneck in the basic CUDA trapezoidal rule?
flashcards.back: atomicAdd serializes concurrent writes to a single global memory location. With N threads all targeting the same address, only one can complete its read-modify-write at a time, making the accumulation effectively serial with O(N) time complexity and high global memory latency per operation.

flashcards.front: How does tree-structured shared memory reduction work in the improved CUDA trapezoidal kernel?
flashcards.back: Each thread loads its value into shared memory. In each step, the stride halves (blockDim/2, blockDim/4, ..., 1) and threads with threadIdx < stride add the value at sdata[tid + stride] to their own sdata[tid]. A __syncthreads() barrier follows each step. After log2(blockDim) steps, sdata[0] holds the total block sum.

flashcards.front: Why is __syncthreads() mandatory between each step of the shared memory reduction?
flashcards.back: Each reduction step reads values written by the previous step. Without __syncthreads(), a thread might read a shared memory location before another thread has finished writing its updated value, causing a race condition. The barrier ensures all threads complete the current step before any thread begins the next.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between stride-halving and stride-doubling reduction patterns?
flashcards.back: Stride-halving (recommended): starts at blockDim/2 and halves each step; threads 0 to s-1 are active, accessing adjacent memory. Stride-doubling (older): starts at 1 and doubles; uses modulo to select threads, causing warp divergence and shared memory bank conflicts. Both produce correct results, but stride-halving is much faster.

flashcards.front: How are block-level partial sums combined after the shared memory reduction?
flashcards.back: Thread 0 of each block writes sdata[0] to blockSums[blockIdx.x] in global memory. The host copies this small array back via cudaMemcpy, sums the numBlocks values in a simple loop, and multiplies by h. Alternatively, a second kernel can reduce the block sums entirely on the GPU.
